about.title: About Me
about.description: Doctor of Medical Sciences (since 2005), Professor, Corresponding Member of RANS, Highest Category Physician. In medicine since age 13, in surgery since age 14. In 2003, together with Professor Kazanskaya I.V., established a new medical specialty — 'Pediatric Urology-Andrology' — in Russia.
about.approachP1: Over 42 years of practice, I've helped thousands of patients — from newborns to adult men. Every case is unique, and I firmly believe that successful treatment starts with attentive care for the patient.
about.approachP2: My surgeries are performed with a precision comparable to ophthalmic surgery. I use modern microsurgical techniques for cryptorchidism, hydrocele, varicocele, and spermatocele.
about.approachP3: A globally recognized expert in ultrasound diagnostics of the reproductive system in children and adolescents.
about.diplomasSubtitle: Proof of qualification and continuous professional development
about.funFact: I collect hedgehog figurines — the second largest collection in the world (over 5,800 pieces)!
about.opinionText: To be honest, I consider the Russian system of certifications and endless paperwork exceptionally pointless. I've spent 40 years accumulating documents that fill a large suitcase. Only about a third are shown here — in total I have 188 such 'quality marks'.

checklist.title: Does Your Child Need an Andrologist?
checklist.subtitle: Take this simple checklist to understand if you should consult a specialist
checklist.lowText: Most likely, an urgent consultation is not needed. However, preventive check-ups with an andrologist are recommended for all boys at ages 0, 1, 3, 6, 10, 14, and 17.
checklist.medText: Symptoms have been identified that should be shown to a specialist at your convenience. Schedule an appointment.
checklist.highText: Symptoms requiring examination by a pediatric andrologist have been identified. We recommend booking the earliest available appointment.
checklist.disclaimer: This checklist is for informational purposes only and does not replace a medical consultation.
items: One or both testicles are not palpable in the scrotum
Pain in the groin or scrotum
Redness or discharge from the foreskin
The urethral opening is not at the tip of the glans
The child complains of pain during urination
Weak, intermittent, or misdirected urine stream
Delayed or premature onset of puberty
Enlarged breast tissue in a teenage boy
Visible or palpable dilated scrotal veins
